title: A Modern R-proach to Econometrics
text:
Preface
Welcome to A Modern R-proach to Econometrics.
This digital book serves as a comprehensive computational companion to Jeffrey Wooldridge’s Introductory Econometrics: A Modern Approach. As a Teaching Assistant at Alexandria University, I developed this resource to bridge the gap between theoretical econometric concepts and practical data analysis using the R programming language.

About the Author
I am Ahmed H. Darwish, a Teaching Assistant with a Bachelor’s degree in Business Administration, majoring in Applied Statistics. Currently, I am pursuing my Master’s degree and conducting research in the field of econometrics.
My work focuses on:
* Applied Econometrics: Replicating complex academic models to ensure reproducibility.
* Causal Inference: Applying modern frameworks from “The Effect” to real-world data.
* Open Source Contribution: I am a contributor to the wooldridge Python library, and I bring that same rigor to this R-based project.

This project is a work in progress as part of my Master’s thesis portfolio. For collaborations or inquiries regarding remote data analysis, feel free to contact me via GitHub.

title: 1  The Nature of Econometrics and Economic Data

Find the average education level in the sample. What are the lowest and highest years of education?



df_wage |>
  summarise(
    Avg_Educ = mean(educ),
    Min_Educ = min(educ),
    Max_Educ = max(educ)
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Avg_Educ Min_Educ Max_Educ
1    12.56        0       18


Interpretation:
The average education in this sample is approximately 12.5 years. The range is broad ranging from 0 years (no formal education) to 18 years of education (graduate level)


Find the average hourly wage in the sample. Does it seem high or low?



df_wage |>
  summarise(
    Avg_Wage = mean(wage, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Avg_Wage
1      5.9


\[
\begin{align*}
{Wage}_{2026} &= {Wage}_{1976} \times \dfrac{CPI_{2026}}{CPI_{1976}}\\
&= 5.9 \times \dfrac{325.3}{56.9}\\
&= 33.7
\end{align*}
\]
Interpretation:
The average nominal wage of $5.90 in 1976 translates to approximately $33.73 in 2026 dollars. This adjustment reveals that the workers in the wage1 dataset were significantly better off than the nominal figure suggests, earning a real wage that is competitive even by modern standards.


The wage data are reported in 1976 dollars. Using the Internet or a printed source, find the Consumer Price Index (CPI) for the years 1976 and 2013.


Interpretation:
CPI for 1976 is $56.9 and became $233 in 2013.


Use the CPI values from part (iii) to find the average hourly wage in 2013 dollars. Now does the average hourly wage seem reasonable?


\[
\begin{align*}
{Wage}_{2013} &= {Wage}_{1976} \times \dfrac{CPI_{2013}}{CPI_{1976}}\\
&= 5.9 \times \dfrac{233}{56.9}\\
&= 24.2
\end{align*}
\] Interpretation:
The average wage is equivalent to $24.2 in 2013, which is three times the minimum wage of $7.25. Minimum wage was $2.3 in 1976 which indicates sample includes skilled labor.


How many women are in the sample? How many men?



df_wage |>
  summarise(
    Count = n(),
    Avg_Wage = mean(wage) |> round(2),
    .by = female
  ) |>
  mutate(Gender = ifelse(female == 1, "Female", "Male")) |>
  select(Gender, Count, Avg_Wage)

  Gender Count Avg_Wage
1 Female   252     4.59
2   Male   274     7.10


Interpretation:
Although sample is well balanced with 252 females and 274 males, there is a notable wage gap between the genders.

How many women are in the sample, and how many report smoking during pregnancy?



df_bwght |>
  summarise(
    Count = n(),
    Smokers = sum(cigs > 0),
    Non_Smokers = sum(cigs == 0)
  )

  Count Smokers Non_Smokers
1  1388     212        1176


Interpretation:
The sample contains 1,388 women. Out of these, 212 women (approximately 15%) reported smoking at least one cigarette per day during pregnancy, while 1,176 reported not smoking at all.


What is the average number of cigarettes smoked per day? Is the average a good measure of the “typical” woman in this case? Explain.



df_bwght |>
  summarise(
    Avg_Cigs_All = mean(cigs),
    Avg_Cigs_Smokers = mean(cigs[cigs > 0])
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Avg_Cigs_All Avg_Cigs_Smokers
1         2.09            13.67


Interpretation:
The sample average is 2.09 because most of the pregnant females are non smokers. A smoker pregnant female smokes on average 13.67 cigarettes per day.


Among women who smoked during pregnancy, what is the average number of cigarettes smoked per day?


Interpretation:
A smoker pregnant female smokes on average 13.67 cigarettes per day.


Find the average of fatheduc in the sample. Why are only 1,192 observations used to compute this average?



df_bwght |>
  summarise(
    Avg_Fath_Educ = mean(fatheduc, na.rm = TRUE),
    Missing_Obs = sum(is.na(fatheduc))
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Avg_Fath_Educ Missing_Obs
1         13.19         196


Interpretation:
An average father has 13.19 years of education in the sample, with 196 missing data.


Report the average family income and its standard deviation in dollars.



df_bwght |>
  summarise(
    Avg_Fam_Inc = mean(faminc, na.rm = TRUE),
    SD_Fam_Inc = sd(faminc, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Avg_Fam_Inc SD_Fam_Inc
1       29.03      18.74


Interpretation:
Families included in the sample have an average income of $29,000 with a standard deviation of $18,700

Find the largest and smallest values of math4. Does the range make sense? Explain



df_meap1 |>
  summarise(
    Max_math4 = max(math4),
    Min_math4 = min(math4)
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Max_math4 Min_math4
1       100         0


Interpretation:
The sample includes math scores ranging from 0 to 100 indication wide variety.


How many schools have a perfect pass rate on the math test? What percentage is this of the total sample?



df_meap1 |>
  summarise(
    Total_Schools = n(),
    Perfect_Math_Count = sum(math4 == 100, na.rm = TRUE),
    Percentage = (Perfect_Math_Count / Total_Schools) * 100) |>
  round(2)

  Total_Schools Perfect_Math_Count Percentage
1          1823                 38       2.08


Interpretation:
Out of the 1,823 schools in the sample, 38 schools (approximately 2.08%) achieved a perfect 100% pass rate on the fourth-grade math test.


How many schools have math pass rates of exactly 50%?



df_meap1 |>
  summarise(
    Schools_at_50 = sum(math4 == 50, na.rm = TRUE)
)

  Schools_at_50
1            17


Interpretation:
There are exactly 17 schools with a pass rate of exactly 50%


Compare the average pass rates for the math and reading scores. Which test is harder to pass?



df_meap1 |>
  drop_na(math4, read4) |> 
  summarise(
    Avg_Math = mean(math4),
    Avg_Read = mean(read4)
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Avg_Math Avg_Read
1    71.91    60.06


Interpretation:
The average pass rate for math 71.90% is significantly higher than for reading 60.06%. This shows that the reading exam was harder in the sample.


Find the correlation between math4 and read4. What do you conclude?



df_meap1 |>
  drop_na(math4, read4) |> 
  summarise(
    Correlation = cor(math4, read4)
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Correlation
1        0.84


Interpretation:
The correlation between the two exams is high 0.84 showing that performance in the two exams is similar.


The variable exppp is expenditure per pupil. Find the average of exppp along with its standard deviation. Would you say there is wide variation in per pupil spending?



df_meap1 |>
  drop_na(math4, read4) |> 
  summarise(
    Mean_Expnd = mean(exppp),
    Std_Expend = sd(exppp)
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Mean_Expnd Std_Expend
1    5194.87    1091.89


Interpretation:
The average expenditure per pupil is $5,194.87, with a standard deviation of $1,091.89 representing a significant spread (21% of the mean).






Note



To know if the spread is significant or not, calculate coefficient of variation (cv).
Small (cv < 10%)
Medium (cv 10% - 30%)
Large(cv > 30%), mean is not reliable here.


\[
CV = \dfrac \sigma \mu
\]


Suppose School A spends $6,000 per student and School B spends $5,500 per student. By what percentage does School A’s spending exceed School B’s? Compare this to \(100 \times [\log(6,000) – \log(5,500)]\)?



school_a <- 6000
school_b <- 5500


pct_diff <- ((school_a - school_b) / school_b) * 100
log_diff <- 100 * (log(school_a) - log(school_b))


results <- c(Exact_Pct = pct_diff, Log_Approx = log_diff)
results |> round(2)

 Exact_Pct Log_Approx 
      9.09       8.70 


Interpretation:
School A spends 9.09% more per student than School B. The logarithmic approximation (\(100 \times \Delta \log\)) yields 8.70, which is a reasonably close estimate.






Important



Logarithmic approximation fails when percentage change is large (> 10%)

Find the average participation rate and the average match rate in the sample of plans



df_401k |>
  summarise(
    Avg_Part_Rt = mean(prate),
    Avg_Match_Rt = mean(mrate)
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Avg_Part_Rt Avg_Match_Rt
1       87.36         0.73


Interpretation:
On average, 87.36% of eligible employees participate in their company’s 401(k) plan. Their company matches $0.73 for every dollar an employee contributes.

estimate the simple regression equation \[\widehat{prate} = \hat \beta_0 + \hat \beta_1 mrate\]


model_401k <- lm(prate ~ mrate, data = df_401k)

modelsummary(
  model_401k,
  fmt = 2,
  statistic = NULL,
  gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared"),
  coef_rename = c("mrate" = "Match Rate ($)", "(Intercept)" = "Constant")
)



    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                 
                (1)
              
        
        
        
                
                  Constant
                  83.08
                
                
                  Match Rate ($)
                  5.86
                
                
                  Num.Obs.
                  1534
                
                
                  R2
                  0.075
                
        
      
    



Interpretation: \[
\beta_0 = 83.03 \quad \beta_1 = 5.86 \quad R^2 = 0.075 \quad n = 1534 \quad
\]


Interpret the intercept in your equation. Interpret the coefficient on mrate


Interpretation:
If the match rate is 0, predicted participation rate is 83.08%.
For ever $1 increase in match rate, participation is predicted to increase by 5.86 percentage points.


Find the predicted prate when mrate = 3.5. Is this a reasonable prediction?



new_data <- data.frame(mrate = 3.5)
pred_prate <- predict(model_401k, newdata = new_data)
round(pred_prate, 2)

     1 
103.59 


Interpretation:
Model predicts a participation rate of 103.59% which exceeds maximum possible value of 100%


How much of the variation in prate is explained by mrate? Is this a lot in your opinion?


Interpretation:
Match rate explains only about 7.5% of the variation in participation, while the remaining 92.5% are due to other factors.

Find the average salary and the average tenure in the sample.



df_ceosal2 |>
  summarise(
    Avg_Salary = mean(salary),
    Avg_Tenure = mean(ceoten)
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Avg_Salary Avg_Tenure
1     865.86       7.95


Interpretation:
Average salary is $865,860 with an average tenure of 7.95 years.


How many CEOs are in their first year as CEO? What is the longest tenure as a CEO?



df_ceosal2 |>
  summarise(
    Fresh_CEO = sum(ceoten == 0),
    Veteran = max(ceoten)
  )

  Fresh_CEO Veteran
1         5      37


Interpretation: 
There are 5 CEOs in their first year, and the longest serving CEO has been in charge for 37 years.


Estimate the regression model \[ \log(salary) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 ceoten + u\]



model_ceosal2 <- lm(lsalary ~ ceoten, data = df_ceosal2)

modelsummary(
  model_ceosal2,
  fmt = 2,
  statistic = NULL,
  gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared"),
  coef_rename = c("ceoten" = "Tenure", "(Intercept)" = "Constant")
)



    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                 
                (1)
              
        
        
        
                
                  Constant
                  6.51
                
                
                  Tenure
                  0.01
                
                
                  Num.Obs.
                  177
                
                
                  R2
                  0.013
                
        
      
    



Interpretation:
For every 1 additional year of tenure as CEO, salary is predicted to increase by 1%






Note



Looks like CEO aren’t driven by loyalty, neither should you

Estimate the model and reprot results



model_sleep75 <- lm(sleep ~ totwrk, data = df_sleep75)

modelsummary(
  model_sleep75,
  fmt = 2,
  statistic = NULL,
  gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared"),
  coef_rename = c("totwrk" = "Minutes Worked per Week", "(Intercept)" = "Constant")
)



    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                 
                (1)
              
        
        
        
                
                  Constant
                  3586.38
                
                
                  Minutes Worked per Week
                  -0.15
                
                
                  Num.Obs.
                  706
                
                
                  R2
                  0.103
                
        
      
    



Interpretation:  A jobless person is predicted to sleep 3586 minutes per week [8.54 hours of daily sleep].
For every 100 minutes worked, 15 minutes of sleep are lost.


If totwrk increases by 2 hours, by how much is sleep estimated to fall?



sleep_fall <- coef(model_sleep75)["totwrk"] * 120

paste("Estimated change in sleep:", round(sleep_fall, 2), "minutes")

[1] "Estimated change in sleep: -18.09 minutes"


Interpretation:  Working an extra two hours per week will reduce weekly sleep by 18 minutes [2.6 minutes per night]. This is practically insignificant.

2.0149031







What is the most likely sign for \(\beta_2\)? \[
bwght = \beta_0 + \beta_1 cigs + \beta_2 faminc + u
\]


Interpretation:  \(\beta_2\) is expected to be positive. Higher family income \(\rightarrow\) better nutrition \(\rightarrow\) higher birth weight


Do you think cigs and faminc are likely to be correlated?


Interpretation:  Yes, negative correlation. smoking rates is higher among lower-income demographics. Omitting family income will cause Omitted Variable Bias (OVB).


Estimate the equation with and without faminc



model_bwght_simple <- lm(bwght ~ cigs, data = df_bwght)
model_bwght_multiple <- lm(bwght ~ cigs + faminc, data = df_bwght)

models_bwght <- list(
  "Without Income" = model_bwght_simple,
  "With Income" = model_bwght_multiple
)

modelsummary(
  models_bwght,
  fmt = 2,
  estimate = "{estimate}{stars}",
  statistic = "std.error",
  gof_map = c("nobs", "r.squared"),
  coef_rename = c(
    "cigs" = "Cigarettes/Day", 
    "faminc" = "Family Income ($1000s)", 
    "(Intercept)" = "Constant"
  )
)



    

    

    
    
    
      
        
        
              
                 
                Without Income
                With Income
              
        
        
        
                
                  Constant
                  119.77***
                  116.97***
                
                
                  
                  (0.57)
                  (1.05)
                
                
                  Cigarettes/Day
                  -0.51***
                  -0.46***
                
                
                  
                  (0.09)
                  (0.09)
                
                
                  Family Income ($1000s)
                  
                  0.09**
                
                
                  
                  
                  (0.03)
                
                
                  Num.Obs.
                  1388
                  1388
                
                
                  R2
                  0.023
                  0.030
                
        
      
    



Interpretation:  Adding family income to the model confirms that it is a statistically significant predictor of birth weight (\(p < 0.05\)), though its practical impact is modest. Importantly, including income reduces the smoking coefficient from approximately \(-0.514\) to \(-0.463\). This indicates that the simple regression suffered from omitted variable bias. \[
\text{Bias} = \beta_2 \times \tilde \delta_1
\] 1. \(\beta_2\): Positive, rich people have heavier babies. 2. \(\tilde \delta_1\): Negative, poor people smoke more 3. Effect: Positive \(\times\) Negative = Negative Bias 4. \(\beta_1\) is negative, negative bias will increase it from -0.46 to -0.51






Tip



Times Two Rule:
If the absolute value of the estimate is more than twice its standard error, it is statistically significant at the 5% level.

What is the estimated increase in price for a house with one more bedroom, holding square footage constant?


Interpretation:  Holding square footage constant, adding one more bedroom increases price by $15,200. This estimate is not statistically significant because adding small rooms without adding square footage will make it feel cramped.


What is the estimated increase in price for a house with an additional bedroom that is 140 square feet in size?



base_house <- data.frame(sqrft  = 140, bdrms = 1)
upgraded_house <- data.frame(sqrft  = 280, bdrms = 2)

price_1 = predict(model_hprice, newdata = base_house)
price_2 = predict(model_hprice, newdata = upgraded_house)

price_increase <- price_2 - price_1

paste(round(price_increase, 2))

[1] "33.18"


Interpretation:  The estimates increase is $33,120. This represents the combined value of adding a room that is 140 square feet \[
140 \times 0.13 + 15.2 = 33.2
\]


What percentage of the variation in price is explained by square footage and number of bedrooms?


Interpretation:  63.2% of the variation in house prices are explained by the two variables.


The first house in the sample has sqrft = 2,438 and bdrms = 4. Find the predicted selling price



new_data <- data.frame(sqrft = 2438, bdrms = 4)
price = predict(model_hprice, newdata = new_data)
paste(round(price, 2))

[1] "354.61"


or

result <- model_hprice$fitted.values[1]
paste(round(result, 2))

[1] "354.61"


or

price = coef(model_hprice)["(Intercept)"] +
  (coef(model_hprice)["sqrft"] * 2438) + 
  (coef(model_hprice)["bdrms"] * 4)
paste(round(price, 2))

[1] "354.61"


Interpretation:  Predicted selling price is $354,000


The actual selling price of the first house in the sample was $300,000 Find the residual for this house.



residual <- model_hprice$residuals[1]
paste(round(residual, 2))

[1] "-54.61"


Interpretation:  Residual is $-54,610. The buyer underpaid for the house.

Interpretation:  \(\beta_4\) = 0.01, one additional year of tenure is associated with 1% increase in salary, holding firm performance constant.


Find the sample correlation coefficient between the variables log(mktval) and profits.



df_ceosal2 |>
  summarise(
    Correlation = cor(lmktval, profits)
  ) |>
  round(2)

  Correlation
1        0.78


Interpretation:  Correlation = 0.78. This high correlation between market value and profits indicates multicollinearity. While this does not bias our estimates or inflate the overall \(R^2\), it makes it difficult to isolate the individual partial effects of profits versus market value, likely resulting in inflated standard errors and insignificant \(t\)-statistics for these specific variables.
